doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 1: What is Stowage Planning (p.1-2)
text: The basics of container stowage planning and why it is so important. By Hariesh Manaadiar (Last Updated: March 13, 2021). In view of the recent incidents relating to stack collapses on board and containers lost at sea, this is a refresher on what container stowage planning is and how it works. It is also a reminder to everyone in the shipping chain - exporters, agents, freight forwarders, carriers, documentation teams, vessel operators, and stowage planners - of the importance of providing and using the correct information for the planning and stowage of the vessel. What is stowage planning? In simple terms, it is the act of allocating space on board the ship for containers that have to be loaded from a certain port(s) to be discharged at certain port(s) without those containers having to be rehandled at any of the way ports along the route. This is probably the most important of all ship operation functions and can be quite intensive in terms of activities and functions. Tools required: (1) The scheduled list of ports that the ship will be calling at, in the order of rotation; (2) A summary of the number of containers - size/type/weight of containers and container numbers per port that are planned to be loaded on the ship; (3) A summary of the number of hazardous, reefer and OOG containers per port that are planned to be loaded on the ship; (4) List and summary of containers already on board for ports past your port. For the purposes of this article, the port is considered to be Durban. Definitions: Profile is the cross-sectional view of the entire ship covering both the deck and under-deck of the ship. The profile gives the total view of the stow positions of which containers are to be discharged at which port. The port operations team and stevedores can identify the sequence of loading and which bays the ship must discharge and/or load from looking at this profile.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 3: Profile (Cross-Sectional View) (p.2-3)
text: What is stowage planning? In simple terms, it is the act of allocating space on board the ship for containers that have to be loaded from a certain port(s) to be discharged at certain port(s) without those containers having to be rehandled at any of the way ports along the route. This is probably the most important of all ship operation functions and can be quite intensive in terms of activities and functions. Tools required: (1) The scheduled list of ports that the ship will be calling at, in the order of rotation; (2) A summary of the number of containers - size/type/weight of containers and container numbers per port that are planned to be loaded on the ship; (3) A summary of the number of hazardous, reefer and OOG containers per port that are planned to be loaded on the ship; (4) List and summary of containers already on board for ports past your port. For the purposes of this article, the port is considered to be Durban. Definitions: Profile is the cross-sectional view of the entire ship covering both the deck and under-deck of the ship. The profile gives the total view of the stow positions of which containers are to be discharged at which port. The port operations team and stevedores can identify the sequence of loading and which bays the ship must discharge and/or load from looking at this profile. The profile provides the full cross section of a ship at one glance. The enlarged version of this will be the actual bay itself. Bayplan is the complete cross sectional view file covering both the deck and under-deck of the entire ship, but displayed or printed per bay. Bay: each container vessel is split into compartments which are termed as Bay and depending on the size of the ship it will proceed from 01 to 88 bays, where Bay 01 is the bay towards the Bow (the front) of the ship and Bay 88 is the Stern (the back) of the ship.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 5: Bay Numbering: 20' vs 40' Stows (p.4-4)
text: Odd numbered bays (1, 3, 5 etc) mean that it is a 20' container stow and even numbered bays (2, 4, 6 etc) mean that it is a 40' container stow. Bay 09/11 (10) and Bay 13/15 (14) are used as an example. What you are seeing is the cross-section of the ship both on deck and under deck. Each of the small square blocks represents a 20' unit space.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 6: Row (Athwartship Position) (p.5-5)
text: Row is the position where the container is placed across the width of the ship. It starts with 01 in the center and progresses outwards with odd numbers on the right (starboard) and even numbers on the left (port).

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
topic_title: Tier and Hatch Covers; Deck vs Under-Deck
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 7: Tier and Hatch Covers; Deck vs Under-Deck (p.6-6)
text: Tier denotes at which level the container is placed - basically how high the container is stacked on board. Hatch Covers (the dark intermittent lines in the picture) are the covers that separate the deck from the under-deck. The area above the line is called the deck (the area visible to us when we look at the ship) and the area below the line is called under-deck (the area not visible from outside the ship). Although computers do most of the work currently, the basis on which they work is the tried and tested methods followed for many years. The list of containers to be loaded onboard is segregated by destination. Space is allocated to each container: firstly in the order of destination - the farthest destination at the bottom and the next port of call right on top; secondly in the order of weight - the heaviest boxes at the bottom and lightest at the top. For reasons of lashing and securing containers, a 40' container can sit on top of two 20's, but two 20's cannot sit on top of a 40' (unless it is under deck and surrounded by other containers or within cell guides).

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 10: Destination Color/Letter Codes & Port Rotation (p.7-7)
text: The profile uses alphabets and colors as codes: F for Felixstowe, A for Antwerp, Ae for Antwerp Empty, H for Hamburg, L for Le Havre, R for Rotterdam, and X to indicate that it is a 40' container. The rotation for this vessel is Felixstowe, Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg and Rotterdam. Because Felixstowe is the first port of call after Durban, containers bound for Felixstowe are stacked right on top of other containers, followed by containers meant for Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg and Rotterdam. Rotterdam is the last port of call hence it is right at the bottom of the heap. In this fashion, the entire ship is filled with the containers to be loaded at each load port while also taking into account containers already on board from previous ports. A container in stow position 110910 means Bay 11, Row 09, Tier 10. By reading the bay plan, one can identify a stow position and where a particular container is located.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 11: Stow Position Reading (Bay-Row-Tier) (p.7-10)
text: The profile uses alphabets and colors as codes: F for Felixstowe, A for Antwerp, Ae for Antwerp Empty, H for Hamburg, L for Le Havre, R for Rotterdam, and X to indicate that it is a 40' container. The rotation for this vessel is Felixstowe, Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg and Rotterdam. Because Felixstowe is the first port of call after Durban, containers bound for Felixstowe are stacked right on top of other containers, followed by containers meant for Antwerp, Le Havre, Hamburg and Rotterdam. Rotterdam is the last port of call hence it is right at the bottom of the heap. In this fashion, the entire ship is filled with the containers to be loaded at each load port while also taking into account containers already on board from previous ports. A container in stow position 110910 means Bay 11, Row 09, Tier 10. By reading the bay plan, one can identify a stow position and where a particular container is located. Worked example (Bay 09 and Bay 11/10): In the bay plan image, the L container circled in position 110910 (Bay 11, Row 09, Tier 10) illustrates how a single stow position is read. Above the hatch cover line is deck stow; below it is under-deck stow. The diagram shows F/A/Ae on deck and H/L/R distributed by destination order with Rotterdam at the bottom.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 12: Restow: Definition, Cause & Cost (p.8-8)
text: A restow is a move where a container is offloaded from on board the ship and put back onto the ship either at the same stow position or a different stow position. This could be due to incorrect stowage of a container - say loaded wrongly for Le Havre instead of Felixstowe - or a change of destination requested at a later stage. To reach a wrongly stowed container, all 12 containers meant for Antwerp (A and Ae) have to be restowed because Antwerp is the next port after Felixstowe. Then the hatch cover has to be opened to reach under deck. Then the 1 container to Le Havre (L) in position 110912 must be restowed as well, and only then can the container in position 110910 be discharged in Felixstowe. This involves considerable cost and wastage of time to restow 12x40' containers and 1x20' container to discharge one incorrectly stowed container. To avoid these costs and wastage of time, it is imperative that the right destination, correct weight, and hazardous cargo information if any is accurately passed onto the ship.

doc_title: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning
citation: The Basics of Container Stowage Planning (Manaadiar 2021), Topic 13: Deck Stress / Tier Weight & Stability (p.9-9)
text: Each of the bays has a deck stress or tier weight which is the maximum allowed weight that each tier/row can carry as per the design of the ship. For example, if there are about 4 containers in a tier each weighing 26 tons, it may not be possible to accommodate all 4 in one tier as this might affect stability due to the heavy nature of the cargo. However, if there are 5 tiers of empty containers as shown in Bay 15, it might be possible to load. These calculations are performed by the computer and show up as errors. Some of the most commonly used software for ship planning are CASP, MACS3 and Bulko. These use the BAPLIE file format structured by UN/EDIFACT. A lot of container stowage is done in centralised hubs these days. Correct stowage planning and proper lashing of containers on board is of utmost importance to avoid maritime disasters.
